skill.name: execution-engine-analysis
skill.description: Analyze control flow, concurrency models, execution topology, event architecture, observability hooks, and scalability traits in agent frameworks.
skill.summary: Map how an agent framework executes work, handles concurrency, emits events, and exposes observability hooks.
skill.author: Dowwie

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found them inside markdown examples, metadata, and analysis terminology. No executable scripts, prompt injection text, network access, filesystem access, credential handling, or malicious intent were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static Command Matches Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are false positives. The matched lines are fenced Python examples and inline indicators that describe async execution patterns, not runnable Ruby or shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is markdown documentation with Python snippets and output templates. No file contains executable shell, Ruby backticks, or instructions to run host commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections do not correspond to encryption, hashing, or credential logic. The matched lines contain descriptive metadata and template wording for architecture analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic APIs, weak algorithms, secrets, or encoded payloads at those locations. The static pattern appears to match ordinary words in documentation.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Matches Are Analysis Terms
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections are false positives. The matched lines ask the user to identify architecture models and classify hybrid execution, not inspect the local system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are part of a process checklist and section heading. They contain no host enumeration commands, environment inspection, or data collection instructions.

content.value_statement: Agent frameworks often hide execution flow, concurrency behavior, and observability limits. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a structured architecture review.
actual_capabilities: Classifies execution models as native async, sync-with-wrappers, or hybrid.
Identifies execution topology as DAG, finite state machine, or linear chain.
Maps callback, event listener, emitter, and async generator patterns.
Inventories observability hooks for prompts, tools, steps, errors, and outputs.
Produces a structured scalability assessment with blocking operations and resource limits.
limitations: Requires source code or framework documentation to produce grounded results.
Does not execute benchmark tests or measure runtime performance directly.
Does not replace a full security review of tool execution paths.
May need companion mapping skills to locate relevant files in large repositories.

use_cases.title: Review a New Agent Framework
use_cases.description: Compare the framework execution model before adopting it for production agents.

use_cases.title: Document Internal Agent Runtime Behavior
use_cases.description: Create clear architecture notes about async support, event hooks, and control flow.

use_cases.title: Compare Framework Scalability Tradeoffs
use_cases.description: Evaluate blocking calls, streaming support, and concurrency limits across options.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Execution Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this agent framework execution engine. Classify its concurrency model, topology, event architecture, and main observability hooks.

prompt_templates.title: Async Model Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the provided execution files. Identify native async, sync wrapper, and hybrid patterns, then explain scalability risks.

prompt_templates.title: Observability Hook Inventory
prompt_templates.prompt: Map every pre-step, post-step, pre-tool, post-tool, LLM, streaming, and error hook. State whether each hook can modify flow.

prompt_templates.title: Framework Comparison Matrix
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these agent frameworks by concurrency model, topology, event system, observability depth, streaming support, and recommended production fit.

output_examples.input: Analyze a framework that exposes invoke and ainvoke methods with callback hooks.
output_examples.output: The result identifies a hybrid concurrency model, a likely linear chain topology, fixed callback hooks, and partial async scalability.

output_examples.input: Review an agent runtime that uses graph nodes and event emitters.
output_examples.output: The result classifies the topology as DAG-based, notes dynamic event registration, and highlights traceability challenges.

output_examples.input: Compare two agent frameworks for streaming and observability.
output: Framework A supports async generator streaming with high traceability.
Framework B uses fixed callbacks with clearer control but lower flexibility.
The comparison recommends the better fit for concurrent workloads.

best_practices: Provide entry point files, executor classes, callback modules, and streaming code together.
Ask for file and symbol references so results stay tied to evidence.
Run codebase mapping first when repository structure is unfamiliar.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for conclusions without providing source code or documentation.
Do not treat marketing claims about async support as implementation evidence.
Do not use this skill as a replacement for runtime load testing.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes execution flow, concurrency style, topology, event architecture, observability hooks, and scalability traits in agent frameworks.

faq.question: Does it run the framework code?
faq.answer: No. It reviews code and documentation to produce an architecture assessment.

faq.question: Can it compare multiple frameworks?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide each framework source or documentation and ask for a structured comparison.

faq.question: Does it find security vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: It can surface execution and hook risks, but it is not a full security scanner.

faq.question: What input works best?
faq.answer: Executor files, graph builders, callback handlers, streaming functions, and framework documentation give the best results.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The skill is suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
